flashcards.back: HCI is a multidisciplinary field focusing on the design, implementation, and evaluation of interactive computing systems for human use, bridging computer science, cognitive psychology, design, and anthropology.
An interaction paradigm is a fundamental model of how users communicate with computers (e.g., command-line, GUI, WIMP, touch, voice, gesture-based).

flashcards.front: List the four basic interaction styles
flashcards.back: 1) Command Language, 2) Menu Selection, 3) Form-Fill-in, 4) Direct Manipulation

flashcards.front: What are the six usability goals?
flashcards.back: 1) Effectiveness, 2) Efficiency, 3) Safety, 4) Utility, 5) Learnability, 6) Memorability
The time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and size of the target: T = a + b log2(D/S + 1). Smaller and farther targets are harder to select.
The time it takes to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number and complexity of choices. T = a + b log2(n + 1)
Affordance refers to the perceived and actual properties of an object that determine how it could possibly be used (e.g., a button appears clickable).
A mental model is a user's internal representation of how a system works, formed through experience and helping users predict system behavior.
A style of interaction where users act directly on objects on screen (drag, drop, click) with continuous visual feedback, making tasks intuitive.
1) Proximity, 2) Similarity, 3) Continuity, 4) Closure, 5) Figure-Ground relationship

flashcards.front: What is Visibility in interaction design?
flashcards.back: Visibility refers to making system functions and states visible to users so they can understand available actions and current system status.
UX encompasses all aspects of a user's interaction with a system, including emotions, beliefs, preferences, perceptions, and responses before, during, and after use.
Feedback is the return of information about an action, confirming that the system received input (e.g., button highlight, loading spinner).
An evaluation method where experts simulate user tasks step-by-step to identify usability problems, focusing on learnability and task completion.

flashcards.front: What is the Golden Rules of interface design?
flashcards.back: 1) Place users in control, 2) Reduce users' memory load, 3) Make interface consistent. These guide design decisions.
